Article.ArticleTitle: Electrochemical induction of high performance electricity generation by novel marine electroactive Rossellomorea aquimaris MT01.

Abstract.AbstractText: Electroactive microorganisms (EAMs) critically govern biogeochemical cycling at the marine sediment-water interface, a key zone for carbon and mineral turnover; however, their efficacy in natural settings is often constrained by inherent instability and limited performance under oligotrophic and dynamically fluctuating environmental conditions. To address this, this study focused on isolating robust EAMs from such challenging habitats, and reports here the isolation of a novel, highly electroactive Gram‑positive bacterium, Rossellomorea aquimaris MT01, from coastal marine sediments. In electrochemical reactors, this strain demonstrated outstanding electrogenic performance, producing a remarkable maximum current density of 2.4 μA/cm2, which notably exceeded that of the benchmark model organism Shewanella oneidensis MR‑1 (1.5 μA/cm2). Furthermore, Rossellomorea aquimaris MT01 sustained significant electrogenic activity under simulated low‑nutrient conditions, highlighting its metabolic flexibility and resilience. Through an integrated multi‑omics approach employing electrochemical techniques, chemical assays, and genome‑resolved transcriptomics, its extracellular electron transfer pathways were systematically investigated, and key regulatory responses to redox variation were unveiled. These findings not only advance the fundamental understanding of electron flux mechanisms and microbial adaptation strategies within marine sediments but also highlight the significant potential of leveraging resilient gram‑positive EAMs for applied biogeochemical engineering and next‑generation environmental biotechnology.
